shortDescription: For resident individuals and HUFs with presumptive income under Sections 44AD, 44ADA, 44AE, or 44B — with income from up to 2 house properties and/or LTCG u/s 112A within ₹1.25 lakh.
longDescription: ITR-4 (Sugam) is a simplified return form for individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) who opt for presumptive taxation under specified sections of the Income-tax Act. It eliminates the need to maintain detailed books of accounts. For FY 2025-26, the form has been significantly expanded to allow up to 2 house properties and LTCG u/s 112A reporting within the exemption limit.
incomeLimitNote: Total income from all sources must not exceed ₹50,00,000
residencyNote: Only RESIDENT individuals and HUFs can use ITR-4. NRIs cannot file ITR-4 (exception: 44AE for goods transport may be available to NRIs — verify with AO).

presumptiveSchemes.name: Presumptive Taxation for Businesses
presumptiveSchemes.eligibleFor: Individuals, HUFs, and Partnership Firms (but ITR-4 is only for individuals/HUF — firms use ITR-5)
presumptiveSchemes.turnoverLimitNote: ₹3,00,00,000 if cash receipts/payments do not exceed 5% of total turnover/gross receipts. Otherwise ₹2,00,00,000.
presumptiveSchemes.presumptiveRate: 8% of turnover (6% if turnover is received via account payee cheque/draft/digital modes)
presumptiveSchemes.incomeClaimed: Net income = 8% or 6% of gross turnover (minimum declared — can declare higher)
exclusions: Persons carrying on profession as specified in Section 44AA(1)
Persons whose business is covered under 44AE (transport)
Persons who previously opted out of 44AD — cannot re-enter for 5 years
Persons claiming deduction u/s 10A, 10AA, 10B, 10BA or 80HH to 80RRB
presumptiveSchemes.eligibleBusinesses: Any business EXCEPT professions specified u/s 44AA(1) and those covered by 44AE

presumptiveSchemes.name: Presumptive Taxation for Specified Professionals
presumptiveSchemes.eligibleFor: Resident individuals only (not HUF, not firms)
presumptiveSchemes.grossReceiptsLimitNote: ₹75,00,000 (increased from ₹50 lakh to ₹75 lakh by Finance Act 2023). Enhanced limit applies if cash receipts ≤ 5% of gross receipts, otherwise ₹50 lakh.
presumptiveSchemes.incomeClaimed: Net income = 50% of gross receipts (minimum — can declare higher)
presumptiveSchemes.legalReference: Section 44ADA, Income-tax Act 1961 (introduced by Finance Act 2016)

presumptiveSchemes.name: Presumptive Taxation for Goods Transport Vehicles
presumptiveSchemes.eligibleFor: Any person who owns goods transport vehicles (not more than 10 at any time during the FY)

presumptiveIncomePerVehicle.heavyVehicle: ₹1,000 per ton of gross vehicle weight per month (or part thereof)
presumptiveIncomePerVehicle.otherVehicle: ₹7,500 per vehicle per month (or part thereof)

presumptiveSchemes.note: Person cannot claim deduction u/s 30 to 38 if using 44AE

presumptiveSchemes.name: Presumptive Taxation for Non-Resident Shipping Business
presumptiveSchemes.eligibleFor: Non-resident taxpayers engaged in shipping business (not applicable for ITR-4 which is for resident individuals/HUF only)
presumptiveSchemes.note: 44B filers are non-residents and typically cannot use ITR-4. Listed here for completeness.

eligibilityCriteria.detail: Must be resident in India for the full year or satisfy residential conditions u/s 6. NRIs cannot use ITR-4.
At least one income source must be under the presumptive taxation scheme. Non-presumptive business income requires ITR-3.

eligibilityCriteria.criterion: Total income from all sources ≤ ₹50 lakh
eligibilityCriteria.detail: If total income (business + HP + other sources + salary if any) exceeds ₹50 lakh, must use ITR-3.

eligibilityCriteria.criterion: Up to 2 house properties (NEW for FY 2025-26)
eligibilityCriteria.detail: ITR-4 now allows income from up to 2 house properties. If more than 2 properties are owned, must use ITR-3. This is an expansion from FY 2024-25 when only 1 property was allowed.
eligibilityCriteria.legalReference: CBDT Notification, ITR-4 Form for AY 2026-27

eligibilityCriteria.criterion: LTCG u/s 112A within ₹1.25 lakh (NEW for FY 2025-26)
eligibilityCriteria.detail: ITR-4 now allows reporting of Long-Term Capital Gains on listed equity/equity MF/business trust units (u/s 112A) provided the net LTCG does not exceed ₹1,25,000. If LTCG exceeds this limit or any capital losses are being carried forward, must use ITR-2/ITR-3.
eligibilityCriteria.legalReference: CBDT Notification, ITR-4 Form for AY 2026-27

exclusionCriteria.detail: NRIs cannot use ITR-4 under any circumstances.
Ownership of 3 or more house properties disqualifies ITR-4.
If any capital loss from prior years is being carried forward and set off, cannot use ITR-4.

exclusionCriteria.criterion: LTCG u/s 112A exceeding ₹1.25 lakh or any other capital gain type
exclusionCriteria.detail: Any STCG, or LTCG on property, or LTCG u/s 112A above ₹1.25L disqualifies ITR-4.
Even a nominee director disqualifies ITR-4.
Holding equity shares of any unlisted company at any time during the FY disqualifies.
Any foreign bank account, foreign investment, ESOP in foreign company, or any income from abroad disqualifies ITR-4.
If aggregate income from all sources exceeds ₹50,00,000.

exclusionCriteria.criterion: Individual who opted out of 44AD scheme in any of the 5 preceding years
exclusionCriteria.detail: Under Section 44AD(4), if a taxpayer does not declare income u/s 44AD in any FY after having done so, they cannot use the scheme for 5 FYs thereafter.
exclusionCriteria.consequence: Must use ITR-3 for those 5 years (maintaining regular books)

hufNote: HUFs can use ITR-4 if they qualify under 44AD or 44AE. 44ADA is NOT available to HUFs — it is only for resident individuals.

comparisonWithOtherForms.vsITR3: Use ITR-3 if: you maintain regular books of accounts, have income above ₹50L, have more than 2 house properties, have capital gains/losses beyond 112A within limit, or are a director/hold unlisted shares.
comparisonWithOtherForms.vsITR1: ITR-1 cannot be used if you have any business income at all. ITR-4 is for presumptive business/professional income.
comparisonWithOtherForms.vsITR2: ITR-2 is for non-business income with capital gains or multiple house properties. ITR-4 is for presumptive income taxpayers.

commonMisconceptions: Misconception: Anyone with a small business can use ITR-4. Fact: Only if business income is computed under presumptive scheme (44AD/44ADA/44AE). Regular books of accounts requires ITR-3.

Misconception: ITR-4 can be filed for any amount of capital gains. Fact: Only LTCG u/s 112A within ₹1.25L is allowed in ITR-4 for FY 2025-26. Any other capital gain or LTCG above the limit requires ITR-2/ITR-3.
Misconception: A doctor earning ₹80 lakh can use 44ADA. Fact: The gross receipts limit for 44ADA is ₹75 lakh (digital transactions) or ₹50 lakh (cash). ₹80 lakh gross receipts disqualify 44ADA.
Misconception: Opting out of 44AD once is okay — you can come back next year. Fact: If you opt out of 44AD in any FY, you cannot use it for the next 5 FYs (Section 44AD(4)).
Misconception: Cash balance disclosure in Schedule BP is optional. Fact: For FY 2025-26, it is MANDATORY for all ITR-4 filers.
Misconception: HUFs can use 44ADA. Fact: Section 44ADA is only for resident INDIVIDUALS. HUFs must use 44AD or 44AE if they want to use ITR-4.

fy202526Changes.summary: House property eligibility expanded from 1 to 2 properties in ITR-4
LTCG u/s 112A reporting allowed in ITR-4 (within ₹1.25L exemption limit)
Mandatory cash and bank balance disclosure added to Schedule BP
